content:
Yesterday we saw the full picture of the twelve dedication offerings brought by the twelve tribal leaders over twelve days the silver vessels, the golden spoon, the burnt offering, the sin offering, the peace offering, and finally the voice of Yahweh that spoke to Moses from between the cherubim after all the offerings were complete. Each of these offerings pointed to Christ. Each one, if we were to follow it all the way, could fill its own full discourse showing how it points to Christ, what it means for us today, and what God is calling us to offer in response. But time will not permit us to go that deep into every offering, as there is much still ahead in the days to come. So, for each offering we will look at the overview; the shape of the truth, the key connection to Christ, and the word it speaks to our own lives. We begin today with the first item placed before the altar: the silver plate.

Each of the twelve tribal leaders brought one silver plate as part of his offering. The Hebrew word for this plate describes a large, wide, flat dish; open, spread out, and without coverings to hide what it contained. It could not conceal its contents. Everything placed on it was visible, exposed, and laid openly before the face of God. The plate held fine flour mixed with oil the grain offering; but it is the material of the plate itself and its weight that speaks to us first.

Silver in Scripture is the metal of redemption. When Yahweh commanded Moses to take a census of Israel, every man from twenty years old and upward was to pay a half-shekel of silver as a ransom for his life; the shekel of the sanctuary (Exodus 30:11-13). It was not gold, it was not bronze, it was silver; the ransom price, the redemption payment, the cost of life. When Joseph’s brothers sold him into slavery in Egypt, the price was twenty pieces of silver (Genesis 37:28). When Judas betrayed Christ to the chief priests, the price was thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:15) the price of a slave under the Law (Exodus 21:32). Silver is what redemption costs. And silver is what the plate was made of.

This silver plate, open and flat before Yahweh, is a picture of Christ as the redemption price displayed openly before God and man. Romans 3:25 says that God “put forward” Christ as a propitiation through faith in His blood.

The phrase “put forward” means publicly displayed, openly set before all eyes exactly like the flat, open, uncovered plate placed before the altar. The cross was not a secret sacrifice. It was a public humiliation. It was a display before heaven, earth, and the powers of darkness. Galatians 3:1 says: “Before your eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified.” The silver plate, wide and open, nothing hidden, everything exposed; this is the cross. The redemption price displayed for all to see.

The plate did not weigh a round number. It was not 100 shekels or 150 shekels. It was precisely 130 shekels, and that number is not without meaning.

In Genesis 5:3 we read: “When Adam had lived 130 years, he fathered a son in his own likeness, after his image, and named him Seth.” One hundred and thirty is the age of Adam when Seth was born. And Seth is not simply the next name on a list. Seth was the son given to replace Abel, whom Cain had murdered. Eve declared at his birth: “God has appointed for me another offspring instead of Abel” (Genesis 4:25). Through the line of Seth; not through Cain the seed of the woman was traced. Noah came through Seth. Abraham came through Seth. And ultimately, the Messiah came through Seth.

So the number 130 carries a layered message. It is the age of a fallen man; Adam, who had already sinned and already seen the consequences of that sin in the blood of his own son Abel, when a new beginning was given to him.

Not through his own effort. Not through Cain, who represented the self-sufficient, God-rejecting line of fallen humanity. Through Seth; the appointed one, the given one, the one who came after the death and the loss, the one who was it the image of his father, just as Christ was in the image of his Father in Heaven. The silver plate at 130 shekels is saying: out of a fallen man, at the point of his deepest loss, God appoints a seed. Out of the wreckage of Adam’s condition, God traces a line that will arrive at Christ.

Galatians 4:4-5 says: “But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth His Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law.” Born of a woman; the seed of the woman promised in Genesis 3:15. Born into the condition of fallen man in order to redeem fallen man from inside that condition. The 130 shekels of the silver plate declare this: Christ did not arrive from outside the problem and solve it from a safe distance. He entered the condition of the 130-year-old Adam; fallen, grief-worn, living with the consequences of sin - and from inside that condition began the new line, the new beginning, the new creation. 2 Corinthians 5:17: “If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.”

The silver plate was flat, open, and exposed before God. Nothing was hidden. Everything placed on it was visible. This is exactly the image Paul uses when he calls us to offer ourselves to God in Romans 12:1: “I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship.” We are not a ransom; Christ was the ransom, once for all (1 Timothy 2:6). But we are called to be a living sacrifice; openly, willingly, completely laid before God on the plate of our daily lives. Not a death but a life. Not taken unwillingly but offered freely.

And just as the silver plate was publicly visible, the offering of our lives before God will be publicly visible too. When we choose to live for God in a world that does not, we will face what Christ faced at the cross; the open display of shame, the exposure, the humiliation before those around us. Friends who do not understand the choices we make. Family members who find our faith strange or excessive. Colleagues who mock or dismiss what we stand for. This is the open plate placed on the altar. It cannot be hidden. The grain offering on the plate was not covered. We do not carry our offering to God in secret.

But here is the word of encouragement the silver plate brings. The 130 shekels speak of a new beginning born out of loss. Every humiliation we bear for the sake of our calling is not the end of the story. It is the moment Seth is born. It is the moment the new line begins. Philippians 2:8-9 shows this pattern in Christ Himself: He humbled Himself to the point of death on a cross; and therefore, God highly exalted Him. The humiliation came first. The exaltation followed. Peter confirms this for us in 1 Peter 5:6: “Humble yourselves, therefore, under the mighty hand of God so that at the proper time He may exalt you.” The open plate is the path to the glory. The 130 shekels of fallen-man’s condition are the weight that precedes the new beginning. Every small humiliation carried faithfully before God is silver on the altar; not wasted, not unnoticed, not the end. It is the ransom price being displayed, the new creation being formed, and the appointed seed of the next chapter being prepared in the heart of the one who is willing to be laid open before God.

title: Prayer
text: Father, you are the God of redemption; the God who pays the price and puts the payment on full display for all to see. We thank You for Christ, the silver plate of our salvation, publicly displayed on the cross as the propitiation for every fallen person who has lived since Adam’s 130th year. We thank You that the number 130 speaks not of death but of a new beginning; that out of the deepest loss and the most fallen condition, you appoint a seed and begin a new line. Help us today to lay ourselves on the plate; openly, willingly, without hiding. When the open plate of our lives brings humiliation in the eyes of those around us, remind us of Philippians 2:8-9. Remind us that the humiliation came before the exaltation, that the cross came before the crown, and that the 130 shekels of our present condition are the exact weight out of which You bring a new beginning. We offer ourselves as living sacrifices today; not to earn what Christ has already paid for, but because your mercies are worth everything we have to lay on the altar. In Christ’s name, amen.
